Run those together, in its telling, and the company reaches profitability 'somewhere within 2027.' For the second quarter of 2026 it guided revenue to a range of $49.5 million to $52.5 million. The company describes this as a model it keeps hitting quarter after quarter, and says it will stay diligent on operating expenses - including the added costs from the Stereolabs acquisition - to get there. It is a target and a framework, not a reported result.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call; the 2027 timing is a management framework
vault_ref: OUST company page / path to profitability + long-term framework

section_title: Read the gross margin carefully
body_text: The reported gross margin flatters the underlying business, and it is worth stripping apart. Ouster's full-year 2025 gross margin of 49.3% included $22.8 million of new IP-license royalties that cost the company almost nothing to earn. Take those out and an outside read puts the underlying product-only margin closer to 41%. Those royalties are not expected to repeat at that scale: management guided total 2026 royalty revenue to less than $5 million, most of it in the back half of the year, and royalty revenue in the first quarter of 2026 was not material. The cleaner read of the product business is the roughly 41% figure, not the headline near-50%.
qualifier: outside read that strips the royalty line from filed figures
vault_ref: OUST company page / royalty-adjusted gross margin

section_title: The open question: lidar versus vision-only
body_text: The central open question over Ouster - and over the whole camera-and-lidar layer of physical AI - is whether lidar keeps a durable role, or whether a camera-only, vision-first approach shrinks the market for it. Outside estimates already rank this sensing layer near the bottom of the value-capture ladder as a commoditizing one. Ouster's own hedge is not to bet on one modality but to fuse both: native-color lidar built on its in-house silicon, the Stereolabs stereo cameras and neural-depth acquired in February 2026, and edge compute, combined into a single perception stack. Whether that fusion strategy defends the lidar market or the market erodes underneath it is genuinely unresolved.
qualifier: an open question over the sensing layer
vault_ref: OUST company page / vision-vs-lidar route risk

section_title: The quarter, and the largest-ever raise
body_text: Palantir's first quarter of 2026: total revenue of $1.633 billion, up 85% year over year and 16% sequentially - the company's highest reported year-over-year growth rate as a public company. The US business, now 79% of total revenue, grew 104% year over year - passing 100% for the first time since the company's direct public offering - with US commercial up 133% and US government up 84%. Customer count reached 1,007, up 31%. The Rule of 40 score, which adds revenue growth to adjusted operating margin, was 145, and net dollar retention was 150%. On the strength of the US results, management raised full-year 2026 revenue guidance to a range of $7.650-7.662 billion, about 71% growth, which it called its largest-ever full-year guidance raise; the implied full-year Rule of 40 guide is 129.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: PLTR company page / Q1 2026 results and raised guidance

section_title: How it bills - outcomes, not seats
body_text: Palantir bills for multi-year outcomes rather than per-seat licenses or metered token consumption: customers commit contract value tied to deployments of its AI platform. In the first quarter of 2026 it closed $2.4 billion of total contract value bookings, up 61% year over year, including $1.2 billion in US commercial bookings - its third straight quarter above $1 billion there - while trailing-twelve-month US commercial bookings were $4.7 billion, up 115%. Remaining performance obligations were $4.5 billion, up 134%, and total remaining deal value was $11.8 billion, up 98%. One caveat the company flags: remaining performance obligations are mostly its commercial business, because they exclude contracts shorter than 12 months and amounts beyond termination-for-convenience clauses that are common in its government work. There is no separately disclosed AI or AIP revenue line - the AI contribution shows up only through the growth rates and these booking figures, not as its own number.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call and quarterly filing
vault_ref: PLTR company page / billed-unit lens and bookings

section_title: The ontology, and the no-slop zone
body_text: Palantir describes its defensibility as ontology depth plus agentic execution rather than a passive data-network effect. The ontology is a semantic model of an enterprise's data, processes and decisions - the company's chief technology officer called it 'the body of the AI brains' - and its AI platform orchestrates software agents across that ontology with governance the company frames as a 'no-slop zone,' meaning every agent action is authorized, cost-attributed and auditable. Management contrasts this 'operational AI' with generic large-language-model output, arguing that the value comes from work that was not previously feasible, not from automating tasks a company already does. Its own framing is that this is defensible but execution-dependent - the forward-deployed-engineer motion that builds the ontology does not commoditize, but it is talent-intensive, so the moat rests on continued execution rather than on a self-reinforcing data flywheel.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call; the execution-dependent read is the company's own framing
vault_ref: PLTR company page / ontology + agentic-execution moat

section_title: The parts that cut the other way
body_text: Set against the growth are several counterweights, all facts about Palantir's own business and none of them changing what the growth is. Government work is a large and slower-growing share: US government was about 42% of revenue in fiscal 2025 and rose to roughly 53% in the first quarter of 2026, and it grows more slowly than the commercial business; US-government budget and appropriations risk is a named risk factor in the company's annual filing. There is a wide gap between the company's GAAP operating margin - about 46% in the quarter - and its adjusted operating margin of about 60%; that gap is stock-based compensation, and the diluted share count of roughly 2.57 billion is rising. And the forward-deployed-engineer model that builds the ontology moat is talent-intensive and structurally hard to scale. These are the execution-dependent side of an otherwise fast-growing business, stated plainly.
qualifier: from the fiscal 2025 annual filing and Q1 2026 quarterly filing
vault_ref: PLTR company page / execution-dependent caveats

section_title: Named deployments, not demos
body_text: Palantir points to named customer deployments as evidence that its 'operational AI' produces real results rather than demos. Its examples include AIG using the platform for agentic underwriting and claims, GE Aerospace deepening a partnership to deploy agentic AI across production, and work with Moder and Freedom Mortgage on the mortgage process and with Ondas and World View. On the government side, it disclosed a US Department of Agriculture contract of up to $300 million. These are named examples the company put forward as evidence of its operational-AI thesis, not audited aggregate results - the argument it makes is that this kind of governed, ontology-based deployment is what generic AI models cannot do on their own.
qualifier: from the Q1 2026 earnings call
vault_ref: PLTR company page / customer deployment evidence
